title: Life and Death in the American West With 'Lean on Pete' Director Andrew Haigh
format: Sean Fennessey talks to Andrew Haigh about 'Lean on Pete,' low-level horse racing, filming the American West from a European vantage, and the terror every director carries onto set.

films.title: Lean on Pete
films.note: Haigh's adaptation of Willy Vlautin's novel, with Charlie Plummer as an orphan boy walking a quarter-horse from Oregon toward Wyoming. Haigh calls it a reverse-Oregon-Trail story about a kid heading east for stability, not freedom, and resists reading it as a coming-of-age film.

films.title: Weekend
films.note: His breakout; he couldn't raise its sub-$100,000 budget and cites it as proof the money got easier only slowly.

films.title: 45 Years
films.note: The married-couple drama he says made 'Lean on Pete' fundable; rewatching it, he noticed it already had some of the same wide landscape work.

films.title: Loveless
films.note: His pick for last great film he saw: Andrey Zvyagintsev's, whose surname he refuses to attempt. 'Insanely bleak,' and he's jealous of its control.

films: All the Money in the World

All the Money in the World.title: All the Money in the World
